food_name: Bakso (Indonesian beef meatball soup with noodles)
region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia — one of Indonesia's most popular street foods
food_subgroup: Beef meatball soup with noodles and tofu

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Bakso. Closest parent is beef meatball soup with noodles.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meatball soup with noodles (Indonesian bakso style)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Bakso serving is approximately 500g: 200g clear beef broth, 3–4 beef meatballs (~150g), 100g noodles, 50g tofu puffs and fried shallots. Served with sambal and lime.

nutrient_source.title: Bakso (Indonesian beef meatball soup) — FAO/INFOODS SEA; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table and IMR Malaysia MyFCD have entries for beef meatball soup. Per-100g values represent the blended bowl composition. The tapioca starch binder in bakso meatballs contributes carbohydrate — unusual for a meat component. Indonesian bakso uses a distinctive smooth, bouncy meatball texture from the tapioca-beef emulsion.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI driven by noodle component (~GI 50–65) and tapioca starch in meatballs (~GI 70). Beef protein and tofu fat moderate the overall glycaemic response. Estimated composite bowl GI ~55–62. Per-500g bowl delivers approximately 56g carbohydrate; GL per bowl ~32.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from beef broth, soy sauce in preparation, and possible MSG in commercial bakso. A 500g bowl delivers approximately 2,100mg sodium — 87% of the daily limit. The clear broth is the primary sodium source.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension can enjoy bakso occasionally. Leave a significant portion of the broth unconsumed — this alone reduces sodium by approximately 400–600mg. Request reduced seasoning if ordering from fresh-cooked stalls.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Bakso has a moderate carbohydrate load from noodles (11.2g/100g) and tapioca in meatballs. A 500g bowl delivers approximately 56g carbohydrate. The beef protein (8.2g/100g) moderates the glycaemic response. Zinc from beef (2.5mg/100g) supports glucose metabolism.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes can eat bakso as a moderate-GL meal. Request glass noodles (lower GI than yellow noodles) as a substitute. The beef meatballs themselves are moderate-GI due to tapioca. Avoid adding extra kecap manis (sweet soy) which adds significant sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low fat (3.5g/100g) with modest saturated fat from beef (1.2g/100g). Zinc from beef (2.5mg/100g) is important for immune function in autoimmune conditions. Vitamin B12 (0.8mcg/100g) supports neurological health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bakso is a suitable regular meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — low saturated fat, good protein, and meaningful zinc. The clear broth is easy to digest. Add extra lime juice (vitamin C) to the bowl to enhance iron absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso is Indonesia's most beloved street food — smooth, bouncy beef meatballs (made from finely minced beef emulsified with tapioca starch) served in a clear, savory beef broth with yellow noodles or glass noodles, tofu puffs, fried shallots, and a spoonful of sambal and lime. The bakso meatball has a distinctive smooth, elastic texture unlike Chinese meatballs — the tapioca starch creates a springy, bouncy consistency. It is sold from mobile cart vendors (tukang bakso) throughout Indonesia and at Indonesian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie, high-protein soup dish, lower in fat than coconut-milk-based dishes.

confidence_reason: FAO/INFOODS SEA and IMR Malaysia MyFCD used for bakso. Values consistent with known beef meatball and noodle soup composition. Medium confidence as Indonesian-specific bakso formulation (tapioca binding ratio) varies.
notes: The tapioca starch in bakso meatballs (approximately 20–25% of meatball weight) contributes unusual carbohydrate for a meat product — clinically relevant for diabetes management. Indonesian bakso is halal by default at Indonesian food establishments. Zinc content (2.5mg/100g) from beef is notable. Glass noodles (soun) have lower GI than yellow wheat noodles — can substitute to lower GL.
